data.source: State-level datasets including: EPA greenhouse gas emissions; Bureau of Labor Statistics (working hours); U.S. Census Bureau (population metrics); League of Conservation Voters (state environmentalism scores, 1990–2005).
data.wave_or_subset: Balanced panel of all 50 U.S. states from 2007–2013.
data.notes: Balanced panel across 50 states for 2007–2013. Includes a time-invariant environmentalism measure from 1990–2005. Study notes limitations due to short time frame and state-level focus.

method.description: Panel regression analysis of the association between average working hours and state-level CO2 emissions across U.S. states (2007–2013), using fixed effects, random effects, and hybrid models. Models include controls for political, economic, and demographic factors, with robustness and sensitivity checks.
steps: Assemble state-level panel (2007–2013) for CO2 emissions, average working hours, GDP per hour, employed population percentage, total population, energy production, manufacturing share of GDP, average household size, working-age population, regional indicators, and an environmentalism index.
Construct a balanced panel dataset of 350 state-year observations.
Estimate fixed-effects and random-effects panel regressions testing scale and composition effects of working hours on emissions.
Estimate hybrid models and conduct sensitivity analyses to assess robustness.
Interpret coefficients, focusing on elasticity of emissions with respect to working hours and the significance/direction of controls.
method.models: Panel regressions including fixed effects, random effects, and hybrid models; specifications for scale and composition effects.
method.independent_variables: Average working hours; GDP per hour; employed population percentage; total population; energy production; manufacturing percentage of GDP; average household size; working-age population; state environmentalism measure; regional indicators (e.g., Midwest, South).
method.control_variables: GDP per hour; employed population percentage; total population; energy production; manufacturing percentage of GDP; average household size; working-age population; state environmentalism scores; regional dummies.

results.summary: Longer average working hours are positively and significantly associated with higher state-level CO2 emissions. Across multiple model specifications (fixed effects, random effects, and hybrid models), a 1% increase in working hours corresponds to roughly a 0.55%–0.68% increase in emissions. Total population and energy production are positive and significant predictors, while state environmentalism scores are negative and significant. Regional effects are positive and significant for the Midwest and South.

numerical_results.outcome_name: CO2 emissions (scale effect, fixed effects model)

CO2 emissions (composition effect, random effects model)

metadata.original_paper_title: US Working Hours and Carbon Dioxide Emissions
